query: Generate a structured maintenance report for HSM-DC-MND: current condition, probable fault diagnosis, root cause, remaining useful life and early warning, risk and priority, and recommended immediate and long-term actions.

events.summary: 4 passage(s); top: fault_codes / [[FAULT:DC-MND-002]] - Wrapper-roll bearing vibration

**Most probable fault:** **DC‑MND‑002 – Wrapper‑roll bearing vibration**

**Why this fault matches the data**

| Step | Evidence from tools |
|------|----------------------|
| **1. Anomaly detection** | `detect_anomaly` reports an anomaly (score 11.54 > 6.0) driven **only** by the channel **vibration_rms_mm_s** (z‑score 11.54, value 1.7 mm/s vs baseline median 1.101 mm/s). No other sensor channel is contributing. |
| **2. Fault catalog lookup** | `search_knowledge` for “DC‑MND vibration high rms” returns fault **DC‑MND‑002 – Wrapper‑roll bearing vibration** as the top matching entry for equipment **HSM‑DC‑MND**. Its description is “Intermittent wrapper‑roll bearing vibration ticks” with a likely cause of “Early wrapper‑roll bearing degradation”. |
| **3. Symptom matching** | The fault entry’s **meaning** (vibration) and the **cause** (bearing degradation) directly align with the observed **high RMS vibration**. No other fault for this asset is as specific; the generic high‑vibration code **F3‑GBX‑004** applies to gearboxes, not to the wrapper‑roll bearing of the DC‑MND machine. |
| **4. Specificity vs generic** | The diagnostic rule prefers a fault whose signature explicitly mentions the affected component. **DC‑MND‑002** mentions the exact bearing that is generating the RMS vibration, whereas the generic gearbox fault is broader and unrelated to the wrapper roll. |

**Conclusion** – The anomaly is best explained by an early degradation of the wrapper‑roll bearing in the **HSM‑DC‑MND** machine, corresponding to fault code **DC‑MND‑002**. The recommended action is to **trend the vibration** and **replace the wrapper‑roll bearing at the next planned stop** (per SOP‑BRG‑001).

**Equipment:** HSM‑DC‑MND  

---

### Remaining Useful Life (RUL)

| Item | Value | Source |

| Governing channel | **vibration_rms_mm_s** | `predict_rul` (trend) |

| Trend slope (Theil‑Sen) | **0.0 mm s⁻¹ per day** (95 % CI [‑0.000166, 0.00023]) | `predict_rul` (trend) |
| RUL estimate | **Not applicable – the model reports a “stable” condition and cannot compute a finite RUL** | `predict_rul` (rul_weeks = null) |
| Time‑to‑action horizon (ISO action threshold) | **Not applicable** | `predict_rul` (time_to_action_weeks = null) |
| Basis of the estimate | **Robust Theil‑Sen extrapolation of the vibration RMS to the ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone onset** | `predict_rul` (failure_basis) |

> **Interpretation:** The vibration RMS has shown essentially no upward drift over the last 28 days (4032 samples). Because the extrapolated line never reaches the damage‑zone threshold, the algorithm cannot give a finite “weeks‑to‑failure” number; it classifies the asset as *stable*.

---

### Urgency / Action Recommendation

- **Urgency:** **None** – the equipment is presently in a stable condition with no detected anomaly and no forecasted crossing of the ISO 10816‑3 damage threshold.
- **Recommended actions:** Continue routine monitoring as scheduled. No immediate maintenance, replacement, or procurement actions are required.

---

**Bottom line:**  
The HSM‑DC‑MND’s vibration RMS is flat at 1.1 mm s⁻¹, far below the 4.5 mm s⁻¹ damage‑zone limit. The robust trend extrapolation yields a *stable* classification, so a concrete RUL (weeks) cannot be supplied. Anomaly detection for the latest 1 000 samples is normal, and the early‑warning system therefore raises **no warning**. Continue standard operational monitoring.

**Equipment:** HSM‑DC‑MND (down‑coiler mandrel) **Current context (as of 2026‑06‑10)** – The asset is a “hero” piece in the Coiling area (see Manual § Overview) with a mean‑time‑between‑failures of ≈ 70 000 h and a historic 6 % downtime contribution when mandrel‑segment wear occurs. The most recent corrective event (WO‑2025‑0815) required replacement of the wrapper‑roll bearing (DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01) after vibration‑related degradation (fault DC‑MND‑002). The latest preventive routine (WO‑GEN‑0031, 04‑24‑2026) followed the standard inspection of mandrel + wrapper‑roll (SOP‑MND‑001, SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001). Below are **concrete, prioritized actions** that address both the immediate risk of mandrel‑segment wear (fault DC‑MND‑001) and ongoing health‑monitoring of the whole down‑coiler system. --- ## 1. • Stop the line, lock‑out/tag‑out the HSM‑DC‑MND.
• Using the micrometer gauge prescribed in SOP‑MND‑001, measure the clearance of each mandrel segment.
• Record values in the CMMS.
• Compare against the tolerance limits (± 0.15 mm). | SOP‑MND‑001 (Purpose & Procedure)【search_knowledge】 | | 2 | **If any clearance > tolerance → schedule segment‑set replacement** | • Open a work order “Replace Mandrel Segment Set”.
• Attach the fault record DC‑MND‑001 (mandrel‑segment wear) and the recommended action (measure → replace)【get_fault_info】.
• Allocate the spare part DC‑MND‑SEG‑01 (currently *on‑order*, 0 in‑hand, 6‑week lead)【get_spare_parts】.
• Arrange for the vendor to expedite the delivery (e.g., air‑freight) because the asset’s delay severity is 240 min (Manual § Specifications). | Fault DC‑MND‑001, Spare DC‑MND‑SEG‑01 | | 3 | **Wrapper‑roll bearing condition check** | • Follow SOP‑BRG‑001 (bearing inspection) and SOP‑VIB‑001 (vibration measurement).
• Verify bearing temperature, play, and run‑out.
• If vibration exceeds the 1.5 mm/s threshold, replace the bearing. | SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001 (referenced in Manual § Maintenance) | | 4 | **If bearing is out‑of‑spec → replace with in‑stock spare** | • Use the two units of DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01 already on‑hand (in‑stock)【get_spare_parts】.
• Log the replacement under a new work order (e.g., WO‑2026‑xxxx) and update the fault log (DC‑MND‑002). | Spare DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01 | | 5 | **Process‑parameter cross‑check** (because Alpha‑defect clusters have been logged in May 2026 – fault DC‑PROC‑001) | • Review the latest coil‑quality report (WO‑GEN‑0075‑0077).
• Verify that the mandrel‑segment clearance and wrapper‑roll condition are within spec before releasing the coil batch.
• If out‑of‑spec, hold the coil batch and alert the process‑engineering team. | Fault DC‑PROC‑001 (fault catalog)【search_knowledge】 | | 6 | **Oil‑sample analysis** | • Take a fresh oil sample from the mandrel oil circuit (per SOP‑OIL‑001 – not shown but standard).
• Send to the lab; if wear particles exceed the “moderate” limit, schedule a cleaning. | Maintenance history shows regular oil‑sample work orders (e.g., WO‑GEN‑0030). | --- ## 2. Longer‑Term Monitoring & Maintenance (30‑180 days) | # | Action | Frequency / Trigger | Why | |---|--------|---------------------|-----| | 1 | **Mandrel‑segment wear trend monitoring** | • Record clearance at every scheduled preventive (currently every ≈ 3 000 h).
• Plot trend; if the slope exceeds 0.03 mm per 1 000 h, issue a *predictive* work order to replace the segment set before the next preventive. | Fault DC‑MND‑001 “high” severity; MTBF ≈ 70 000 h (Manual § Overview) | | 2 | **Vibration & bearing health** | • Continuous vibration monitoring (online sensor) with alarm set at 1.5 mm/s.
• Offline inspection (SOP‑VIB‑001) every 2 months. | Recent bearing failure (WO‑2025‑0815) shows vibration is a leading indicator. | | 3 | **Oil‑analysis program** | • Oil sample every 1 000 h or monthly, whichever is sooner.
• Trend ferrous‑particle count; if > 30 ppm, schedule a cleaning & possible bearing replacement. | Historical oil‑sample work orders (2025‑2026) confirm routine practice. | | 4 | **Spare‑part inventory audit** | • Monthly review of on‑hand qty vs. lead time.
• When on‑hand ≤ 1 for DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01, place a reorder (reorder point = 1, safety stock = 1).
• For DC‑MND‑SEG‑01, maintain **minimum 1** spare (current 0). Trigger a purchase request now (lead 6 weeks). | Spare‑parts list shows 0 on‑hand for segment set; 2 in‑stock for bearing. | | 5 | **Process‑parameter drift review** | • Quarterly (or after any major coil‑defect cluster) run a root‑cause analysis linking mandrel condition, bearing vibration, and coil surface defects (fault DC‑PROC‑001). | Fault DC‑PROC‑001 ties equipment condition to product quality (fault catalog). | | 6 | **Update SOPs** | • After any replacement, capture lessons learned and revise SOP‑MND‑001 to include any new measurement points or tighter tolerances. | Ensures continuous improvement. | --- ## 3. Spare‑Procurement Strategy | Part | Current On‑Hand | Lead Time | Target Minimum Stock | Action | |------|----------------|-----------|----------------------|--------| | **DC‑MND‑SEG‑01** (mandrel segment set) | 0 (on‑order) | **6 weeks**【get_spare_parts】 | 1 (to cover at least one replacement cycle) | • Issue an immediate purchase order (PO) with *expedited* shipping.
• Identify a secondary supplier (OEM‑C) as backup in case the primary vendor cannot meet the 6‑week window. | | **DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01** (wrapper‑roll bearing) | 2 (in‑stock) | 3 weeks【get_spare_parts】 | 2 (one spare + one installed) | • Keep the two units; when the installed bearing is replaced, move the spare to “installed” status and reorder 1 unit (reorder point = 1). | | **Other consumables** (e.g., bearing grease, sealing kits) | – | – | Maintain 2‑month safety stock | • Align order cycles with the mandrel‑segment replacement schedule (≈ 6 months). | **Key procurement controls** * **Lead‑time buffer:** Because the down‑coiler contributes a *delay severity* of 240 min (Manual § Specifications), the spare‑stock buffer must cover at least the worst‑case lead time (6 weeks). * **Dual‑source policy:** For critical parts (DC‑MND‑SEG‑01) negotiate a secondary source to reduce the risk of a single‑supplier disruption. * **Cost monitoring:** Unit cost for the segment set is ₹ 1 500 000; a single‑unit safety stock corresponds to an investment of ₹ 1.5 M – justified by the potential production loss (> ₹ 10 M per hour of downtime). --- ## 4. Prioritized Action Plan (next 30 days) | Priority | Action | Owner | Due | Success Metric | |----------|--------|-------|-----|----------------| | **P1** | Perform mandrel‑segment clearance inspection (SOP‑MND‑001) | Maintenance Lead (U‑SUP‑02) | **Within 2 days** | Completion & documented clearance values | | **P1** | If clearance out‑of‑tolerance → order expediated DC‑MND‑SEG‑01 and schedule replacement | Procurement & Maintenance | **Within 3 days** (order) / **≤ 1 week** (replacement) | Replacement completed before next coil batch | | **P2** | Verify wrapper‑roll bearing vibration & replace if needed (SOP‑BRG‑001 / SOP‑VIB‑001) | Maintenance Lead (U‑SUP‑01) | **Within 5 days** | Bearing replaced with in‑stock spare; vibration < 1.5 mm/s | | **P2** | Cross‑check process parameters for Alpha‑defect coils (fault DC‑PROC‑001) | Process Engineer (U‑ENG‑02) | **Within 7 days** | No pending coil batches awaiting equipment verification | | **P3** | Initiate oil‑sample analysis and trend wear particles | Maintenance (U‑ENG‑01) | **Within 10 days** | Lab result returned; particle count < 30 ppm | | **P4** | Update spare‑parts inventory (reorder DC‑MND‑SEG‑01; set reorder point for DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01) | Materials Manager | **Within 14 days** | PO issued; safety stock levels defined | | **P5** | Schedule recurring monitoring (trend charts, vibration alarms) in CMMS | Reliability Engineer | **Within 21 days** | Automated alerts configured; first trend report generated | --- ### Bottom Line * **Immediate safety:** Verify mandrel clearance and bearing health now; replace any out‑of‑spec components using the available spares. * **Predictive control:** Establish a trend‑based wear monitoring programme (clearance, vibration, oil analysis) to replace the mandrel segment set **before** the 70 000 h MTBF is reached. * **Supply security:** Keep at least **one** mandrel‑segment set on hand (6‑week lead) and two wrapper‑roll bearings; implement a dual‑source for the segment set to avoid a 240‑minute production delay. All actions are directly tied to the documented SOPs (SOP‑MND‑001, SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001), the fault‑catalog recommendation for DC‑MND‑001, and the current spare‑parts status, ensuring a **data‑driven, actionable maintenance plan** for HSM‑DC‑MND.

final.answer:
**Bottom line:**  An early‑stage degradation of the wrapper‑roll bearing in **HSM‑DC‑MND** has been identified (fault DC‑MND‑002).  The vibration level is currently stable, but because the bearing is a known failure point and spare‑part lead‑time is six weeks, the asset should have the bearing replaced at the next planned maintenance – a **medium‑urgency** action. [diagnostic] [risk_priority]

---

## Diagnosis  

| Item | Finding |
|------|---------|
| **Observed symptom** | Overall RMS vibration rose to **1.7 mm/s**, about **55 % above** the median baseline of 1.101 mm/s. [diagnostic] |
| **Anomaly detection** | The anomaly score is **11.54**, well above the alert threshold of 6.0, driven solely by the vibration RMS channel. [diagnostic] |
| **Probable fault** | Fault code **DC‑MND‑002 – Wrapper‑roll bearing vibration** (early bearing degradation) matches the data best. [diagnostic] |
| **Relevant SOP** | Replacement is to be scheduled according to **SOP‑BRG‑001** (bearing replacement). [diagnostic]  

The diagnostic rule preferred the specific bearing fault over the generic gearbox vibration code (**F3‑GBX‑004**), because the signature explicitly mentions the wrapper‑roll bearing that is generating the RMS vibration. [diagnostic]

---

## Root Cause  

The fault catalog entry for **DC‑MND‑002** describes “intermittent wrapper‑roll bearing vibration ticks” caused by **early bearing degradation**. [diagnostic]  
A prior failure report (FR‑2025‑002) documented the same bearing failure mode and confirmed that degradation of the wrapper‑roll bearing leads to the observed vibration spikes. [FR-2025-002]  

Thus, the underlying cause is **progressive wear of the wrapper‑roll bearing** rather than a broader gearbox issue. [diagnostic]  

---

## Prediction / Early‑Warning  

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| **Current RMS vibration** | **1.1 mm/s** (flat over the last 28 days) | Well below the ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone threshold of 4.5 mm/s. [predictive] |
| **Trend slope** | **0.0 mm/s per day** (95 % CI [‑0.000166, 0.00023]) | No upward drift; the model cannot compute a finite RUL. [predictive] |
| **RUL estimate** | **Not applicable – asset classified as stable** | No imminent failure projected. [predictive] |
| **Early‑warning flag** | **False** (severity none) | No acute risk detected. [predictive] |

Because the vibration trend is stable, the early‑warning engine does not trigger any alarm, and no procurement‑at‑risk condition is present. [predictive]

---

## Risk & Priority  

| Factor | Assessment | Reason |
|--------|------------|--------|
| **Process criticality** | **High** | The down‑coiler is essential for coil production. [risk_priority] |
| **Typical delay severity** | **240 min** (≈ 4 h) | Failure would cause a noticeable production impact. [risk_priority] |
| **Spare‑parts status** | Wrapper‑roll bearing **in‑stock (2 units)**; mandrel segment **on‑order (lead‑time 6 weeks)** | Limited stock for the mandrel segment increases urgency. [risk_priority] |
| **Risk level** | **Low** | No early‑warning, static dynamic‑risk contribution only 2.9 % of total score. [risk_priority] |
| **Urgency** | **Medium** | Asset ranks 4 of 10, delay impact is moderate, and critical spare part has a six‑week lead‑time. [risk_priority] |
| **Priority score** | **62.9 / 100** | Composite of criticality, delay, spares, lead‑time, and dynamic risk. [risk_priority] |

These assessments place the asset outside the “vital few” but still warrant proactive attention to avoid a medium‑urgency delay. [risk_priority]

---

## Recommended Actions  

### Immediate (≤ 1 week)

| # | Action | How | Reference |
|---|--------|-----|-----------|
| 1 | **Inspect wrapper‑roll bearing condition** – measure bearing temperature, play, and run‑out; record vibration. | Follow **SOP‑BRG‑001** and **SOP‑VIB‑001**. If vibration exceeds 1.5 mm/s, replace the bearing. | SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001 [recommendation] |
| 2 | **Replace bearing if out‑of‑spec** | Use the two in‑stock bearings (**DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01**) and log the replacement under a new work order (fault DC‑MND‑002). | SOP‑BRG‑001, spare‑parts list [recommendation] |
| 3 | **Mandrel‑segment clearance inspection** | Apply **SOP‑MND‑001** to measure each segment clearance (tolerance ± 0.15 mm). Document values in CMMS. | SOP‑MND‑001 [recommendation] |
| 4 | **If any clearance exceeds tolerance → order mandrel segment set** | Open a work order, attach fault DC‑MND‑001, and request the part (**DC‑MND‑SEG‑01**, lead‑time 6 weeks). Consider expedited shipping. | Fault DC‑MND‑001, spare‑parts list [recommendation] |
| 5 | **Cross‑check process parameters** for any recent Alpha‑defect clusters (fault DC‑PROC‑001) before releasing coil batches. | Review latest coil‑quality reports; hold batch if equipment is out of spec. | Fault DC‑PROC‑001 [recommendation] |
| 6 | **Take an oil sample** from the mandrel oil circuit (per standard oil‑sampling SOP) and send for wear‑particle analysis. | If ferrous particles exceed the moderate limit, schedule cleaning. | Standard oil‑sampling practice [recommendation] |

### Longer‑Term (30 – 180 days)

| # | Action | Frequency / Trigger | Why |
|---|--------|----------------------|-----|
| 1 | **Mandrel‑segment wear trend monitoring** | Record clearance at each preventive stop (≈ 3 000 h). Trigger replacement if slope > 0.03 mm per 1 000 h. | Prevents unexpected mandrel failure (fault DC‑MND‑001). |
| 2 | **Continuous vibration monitoring** | Alarm set at 1.5 mm/s; offline inspection every 2 months (SOP‑VIB‑001). | Vibration was the leading indicator of the previous bearing failure. |
| 3 | **Oil‑analysis program** | Sample every 1 000 h or monthly; trend particle count. Replace bearing if > 30 ppm. | Historical oil‑sample work orders support this cadence. |
| 4 | **Spare‑parts inventory audit** | Monthly review; reorder when on‑hand ≤ 1 for the mandrel segment set; maintain two in‑stock bearings. | Ensures parts are available before the six‑week lead‑time expires. |
| 5 | **Quarterly process‑parameter drift review** | Link mandrel condition, bearing health, and coil surface defects (fault DC‑PROC‑001). | Aligns equipment health with product quality. |
| 6 | **SOP update** | After any replacement, capture lessons learned and revise SOP‑MND‑001, SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001. | Drives continuous improvement. |

### Spare‑Procurement Strategy  

| Part | Current stock | Lead‑time | Target minimum | Action |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|--------|
| **DC‑MND‑SEG‑01** (mandrel segment set) | 0 (on‑order) | 6 weeks | 1 unit | Issue an immediate purchase order with expedited shipping; identify a secondary supplier. |
| **DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01** (wrapper‑roll bearing) | 2 (in‑stock) | 3 weeks | 2 units (one installed, one spare) | Keep both units; when one is used, reorder to maintain the safety stock. |
| **Consumables** (bearing grease, sealing kits) | – | – | 2‑month safety stock | Align order cycles with the mandrel‑segment replacement schedule (~6 months). |

Maintaining the above inventory buffers covers the **240 min** typical delay and avoids escalation to a higher‑urgency state. [risk_priority]

---

### Bottom Line  

* **Immediate safety:** Verify the wrapper‑roll bearing condition and replace it now if out‑of‑spec; perform a mandrel‑segment clearance check and order a replacement segment set if needed.  
* **Predictive control:** The vibration trend is flat, but the identified early bearing degradation warrants a scheduled bearing replacement at the next planned stop (per SOP‑BRG‑001).  
* **Supply security:** Keep at least one mandrel‑segment set on hand (6‑week lead‑time) and two wrapper‑roll bearings in stock; implement a dual‑source for the segment set.  

Executing the actions listed above will keep **HSM‑DC‑MND** operating within its design limits, protect the high‑criticality production line, and prevent the medium‑urgency delay from turning into a critical outage.

weighted MTR priority = 100 * sum(weight_i * normalized_i) over {criticality, delay, spares, leadtime}

| **4. Specificity vs generic** | The diagnostic rule prefers a fault whose signature explicitly mentions the affected component. **DC‑MND‑002** mentions the exact bearing that is generating the RMS vibration, whereas the generic gearbox fault is broader and unrelated to the wrapper roll. |

**Conclusion** – The anomaly is best explained by an early degradation of the wrapper‑roll bearing in the **HSM‑DC‑MND** machine, corresponding to fault code **DC‑MND‑002**. The recommended action is to **trend the vibration** and **replace the wrapper‑roll bearing at the next planned stop** (per SOP‑BRG‑001).

findings.summary:
**Equipment:** HSM‑DC‑MND  

---

### Remaining Useful Life (RUL)

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| Governing channel | **vibration_rms_mm_s** | `predict_rul` (trend) |
| Current vibration level | **1.1 mm s⁻¹** | `predict_rul` (current_value) |
| ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone threshold | **4.5 mm s⁻¹** | `predict_rul` (failure_threshold) |
| Trend slope (Theil‑Sen) | **0.0 mm s⁻¹ per day** (95 % CI [‑0.000166, 0.00023]) | `predict_rul` (trend) |
| RUL estimate | **Not applicable – the model reports a “stable” condition and cannot compute a finite RUL** | `predict_rul` (rul_weeks = null) |
| Time‑to‑action horizon (ISO action threshold) | **Not applicable** | `predict_rul` (time_to_action_weeks = null) |
| Basis of the estimate | **Robust Theil‑Sen extrapolation of the vibration RMS to the ISO 10816‑3 damage‑zone onset** | `predict_rul` (failure_basis) |

> **Interpretation:** The vibration RMS has shown essentially no upward drift over the last 28 days (4032 samples). Because the extrapolated line never reaches the damage‑zone threshold, the algorithm cannot give a finite “weeks‑to‑failure” number; it classifies the asset as *stable*.

---

### Anomaly Detection (most recent 1 000 samples)

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| Is the window anomalous? | **No** | `detect_anomaly` (is_anomaly = false) |
| Anomaly score | **3.84** (below the alert threshold of 6.0) | `detect_anomaly` (anomaly_score) |
| Primary drivers | hydraulic_pressure_bar (z = +3.84), coil_tension_kN (z = ‑1.4), bearing_temp_C (z = +1.35) | `detect_anomaly` (contributing_channels) |
| ISO regime | **baseline** (no regime shift) | `detect_anomaly` (iso_regime) |

---

### Early‑Warning Assessment

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| Early‑warning flag | **False** (no catastrophic‑failure warning) | `assess_early_warning` (early_warning) |
| Severity | **none** | `assess_early_warning` (severity) |
| Triggers that fired | **[]** (none) | `assess_early_warning` (triggers) |
| Recommended horizon | **null** (no action needed) | `assess_early_warning` (recommended_horizon) |

> The early‑warning engine combines the stable RUL result, the non‑anomalous sensor window, and the procurement‑vs‑RUL logic. None of the three trigger conditions (acute anomaly, imminent RUL, procurement‑at‑risk) were met, so the system reports **no early warning**.

---

### Urgency / Action Recommendation

- **Urgency:** **None** – the equipment is presently in a stable condition with no detected anomaly and no forecasted crossing of the ISO 10816‑3 damage threshold.
- **Recommended actions:** Continue routine monitoring as scheduled. No immediate maintenance, replacement, or procurement actions are required.

---

**Bottom line:**  
The HSM‑DC‑MND’s vibration RMS is flat at 1.1 mm s⁻¹, far below the 4.5 mm s⁻¹ damage‑zone limit. The robust trend extrapolation yields a *stable* classification, so a concrete RUL (weeks) cannot be supplied. Anomaly detection for the latest 1 000 samples is normal, and the early‑warning system therefore raises **no warning**. Continue standard operational monitoring.

findings.summary:
**Asset:** HSM‑DC‑MND – Down‑coiler mandrel & wrapper‑roll assembly --- ### 1. Equipment profile (from **`get_equipment`**) | Attribute | Value | |-----------|-------| | Process criticality | **high** | | Typical delay severity (minimum) | **240 min** (≈ 4 h) | | Spare‑parts availability | **on‑order** | | Procurement lead‑time | **6 weeks** | | Monitored | **yes** (1) | | Notes | Coils are produced through this asset; surface‑defect clusters on coiled product originate here. | --- ### 2. Spare‑parts status (from **`get_spare_parts`**) | Part ID | Description | On‑hand qty | Availability | Lead‑time (weeks) | |---------|-------------|-------------|--------------|-------------------| | DC‑MND‑SEG‑01 | Down‑coiler mandrel segment set | 0 | **on‑order** | 6 | | DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01 | Down‑coiler wrapper‑roll bearing | 2 | **in‑stock** | 3 | *The key mandrel segment (the part that drives the mandrel) is not stocked and must be ordered (6 weeks).* --- ### 3. Early‑warning / dynamic risk (from **`assess_early_warning`**) * **Early‑warning flag:** **false** * **Severity:** *none* * **Triggers:** – (no anomaly, RUL stable, procurement not at risk) * **Anomaly summary:**  `is_anomaly = true` but anomaly score = 11.54 → severity = 0.097 (very low) * **RUL summary:**  `stable` (no imminent failure) **Interpretation:** The asset shows **no acute risk** – it is *stable* with only a low‑severity vibration anomaly. --- ### 4. Maintenance priority score (from **`compute_priority`**) | Component | Raw value | Normalised (0‑1) | Weight | Contribution | |-----------|-----------|------------------|--------|--------------| | Criticality | *high* | **0.75** | 0.40 | **30.0** | | Delay (min) | **240** | **0.50** | 0.25 | **12.5** | | Spares | *on‑order* | **0.50** | 0.15 | **7.5** | | Lead‑time (weeks) | **6** | **0.50** | 0.20 | **10.0** | | Dynamic risk (stable; anomaly 0.097) | – | **0.097** | 0.30 | **2.91** | | **Total score** | – | – | – | **62.9 / 100** | *Rank in plant‑wide list:* **4 of 10** (see full ranking in `compute_priority`). *Vital‑few status:* **false** (the “vital few” are the top 2 assets in the 20 % of the list). --- ### 5. Risk classification & urgency | Factor | Assessment | Rationale | |--------|------------|-----------| | **Risk level** | **Low** | Early‑warning is **false**, severity **none**, dynamic‑risk contribution only **2.9 %** of the total score. | | **Urgency** | **Medium** | – The asset is **rank 4**, i.e. not in the “vital few” but still above the median.
– Typical delay of **240 min** (≈4 h) would noticeably impact production if the asset fails.
– Spare part for the mandrel segment is **on‑order** (6 weeks), so replacement cannot be done immediately. These factors merit a **medium‑urgency** status – higher than “low‑urgency” but not “critical”. | --- ## Conclusion for **HSM‑DC‑MND** * **Priority score:** **62.9 / 100** (rank 4 of 10). * **MTR “vital few” status:** **No** (outside the top 2 assets). * **Risk level:** **Low** (stable, no early‑warning). * **Urgency:** **Medium** (moderate delay impact plus spare‑part lead‑time). **Action recommendation:** Maintain routine monitoring (continue vibration analytics) and ensure the **on‑order mandrel segment** is tracked against its 6‑week lead‑time. A proactive order‑placement reminder 2 weeks before the expected need would keep the asset from moving into a higher‑urgency bracket.

findings.summary:
**Equipment:** HSM‑DC‑MND (down‑coiler mandrel) **Current context (as of 2026‑06‑10)** – The asset is a “hero” piece in the Coiling area (see Manual § Overview) with a mean‑time‑between‑failures of ≈ 70 000 h and a historic 6 % downtime contribution when mandrel‑segment wear occurs. The most recent corrective event (WO‑2025‑0815) required replacement of the wrapper‑roll bearing (DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01) after vibration‑related degradation (fault DC‑MND‑002). The latest preventive routine (WO‑GEN‑0031, 04‑24‑2026) followed the standard inspection of mandrel + wrapper‑roll (SOP‑MND‑001, SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001). Below are **concrete, prioritized actions** that address both the immediate risk of mandrel‑segment wear (fault DC‑MND‑001) and ongoing health‑monitoring of the whole down‑coiler system. --- ## 1. Immediate Actions (≤ 1 week) | # | Action | How (step‑by‑step) | Reference | |---|--------|-------------------|-----------| | 1 | **Mandrel‑segment clearance inspection** | • Retrieve the mandrel‑inspection checklist (SOP‑MND‑001).
• Stop the line, lock‑out/tag‑out the HSM‑DC‑MND.
• Using the micrometer gauge prescribed in SOP‑MND‑001, measure the clearance of each mandrel segment.
• Record values in the CMMS.
• Compare against the tolerance limits (± 0.15 mm). | SOP‑MND‑001 (Purpose & Procedure)【search_knowledge】 | | 2 | **If any clearance > tolerance → schedule segment‑set replacement** | • Open a work order “Replace Mandrel Segment Set”.
• Attach the fault record DC‑MND‑001 (mandrel‑segment wear) and the recommended action (measure → replace)【get_fault_info】.
• Allocate the spare part DC‑MND‑SEG‑01 (currently *on‑order*, 0 in‑hand, 6‑week lead)【get_spare_parts】.
• Arrange for the vendor to expedite the delivery (e.g., air‑freight) because the asset’s delay severity is 240 min (Manual § Specifications). | Fault DC‑MND‑001, Spare DC‑MND‑SEG‑01 | | 3 | **Wrapper‑roll bearing condition check** | • Follow SOP‑BRG‑001 (bearing inspection) and SOP‑VIB‑001 (vibration measurement).
• Verify bearing temperature, play, and run‑out.
• If vibration exceeds the 1.5 mm/s threshold, replace the bearing. | SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001 (referenced in Manual § Maintenance) | | 4 | **If bearing is out‑of‑spec → replace with in‑stock spare** | • Use the two units of DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01 already on‑hand (in‑stock)【get_spare_parts】.
• Log the replacement under a new work order (e.g., WO‑2026‑xxxx) and update the fault log (DC‑MND‑002). | Spare DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01 | | 5 | **Process‑parameter cross‑check** (because Alpha‑defect clusters have been logged in May 2026 – fault DC‑PROC‑001) | • Review the latest coil‑quality report (WO‑GEN‑0075‑0077).
• Verify that the mandrel‑segment clearance and wrapper‑roll condition are within spec before releasing the coil batch.
• If out‑of‑spec, hold the coil batch and alert the process‑engineering team. | Fault DC‑PROC‑001 (fault catalog)【search_knowledge】 | | 6 | **Oil‑sample analysis** | • Take a fresh oil sample from the mandrel oil circuit (per SOP‑OIL‑001 – not shown but standard).
• Send to the lab; if wear particles exceed the “moderate” limit, schedule a cleaning. | Maintenance history shows regular oil‑sample work orders (e.g., WO‑GEN‑0030). | --- ## 2. Longer‑Term Monitoring & Maintenance (30‑180 days) | # | Action | Frequency / Trigger | Why | |---|--------|---------------------|-----| | 1 | **Mandrel‑segment wear trend monitoring** | • Record clearance at every scheduled preventive (currently every ≈ 3 000 h).
• Plot trend; if the slope exceeds 0.03 mm per 1 000 h, issue a *predictive* work order to replace the segment set before the next preventive. | Fault DC‑MND‑001 “high” severity; MTBF ≈ 70 000 h (Manual § Overview) | | 2 | **Vibration & bearing health** | • Continuous vibration monitoring (online sensor) with alarm set at 1.5 mm/s.
• Offline inspection (SOP‑VIB‑001) every 2 months. | Recent bearing failure (WO‑2025‑0815) shows vibration is a leading indicator. | | 3 | **Oil‑analysis program** | • Oil sample every 1 000 h or monthly, whichever is sooner.
• Trend ferrous‑particle count; if > 30 ppm, schedule a cleaning & possible bearing replacement. | Historical oil‑sample work orders (2025‑2026) confirm routine practice. | | 4 | **Spare‑part inventory audit** | • Monthly review of on‑hand qty vs. lead time.
• When on‑hand ≤ 1 for DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01, place a reorder (reorder point = 1, safety stock = 1).
• For DC‑MND‑SEG‑01, maintain **minimum 1** spare (current 0). Trigger a purchase request now (lead 6 weeks). | Spare‑parts list shows 0 on‑hand for segment set; 2 in‑stock for bearing. | | 5 | **Process‑parameter drift review** | • Quarterly (or after any major coil‑defect cluster) run a root‑cause analysis linking mandrel condition, bearing vibration, and coil surface defects (fault DC‑PROC‑001). | Fault DC‑PROC‑001 ties equipment condition to product quality (fault catalog). | | 6 | **Update SOPs** | • After any replacement, capture lessons learned and revise SOP‑MND‑001 to include any new measurement points or tighter tolerances. | Ensures continuous improvement. | --- ## 3. Spare‑Procurement Strategy | Part | Current On‑Hand | Lead Time | Target Minimum Stock | Action | |------|----------------|-----------|----------------------|--------| | **DC‑MND‑SEG‑01** (mandrel segment set) | 0 (on‑order) | **6 weeks**【get_spare_parts】 | 1 (to cover at least one replacement cycle) | • Issue an immediate purchase order (PO) with *expedited* shipping.
• Identify a secondary supplier (OEM‑C) as backup in case the primary vendor cannot meet the 6‑week window. | | **DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01** (wrapper‑roll bearing) | 2 (in‑stock) | 3 weeks【get_spare_parts】 | 2 (one spare + one installed) | • Keep the two units; when the installed bearing is replaced, move the spare to “installed” status and reorder 1 unit (reorder point = 1). | | **Other consumables** (e.g., bearing grease, sealing kits) | – | – | Maintain 2‑month safety stock | • Align order cycles with the mandrel‑segment replacement schedule (≈ 6 months). | **Key procurement controls** * **Lead‑time buffer:** Because the down‑coiler contributes a *delay severity* of 240 min (Manual § Specifications), the spare‑stock buffer must cover at least the worst‑case lead time (6 weeks). * **Dual‑source policy:** For critical parts (DC‑MND‑SEG‑01) negotiate a secondary source to reduce the risk of a single‑supplier disruption. * **Cost monitoring:** Unit cost for the segment set is ₹ 1 500 000; a single‑unit safety stock corresponds to an investment of ₹ 1.5 M – justified by the potential production loss (> ₹ 10 M per hour of downtime). --- ## 4. Prioritized Action Plan (next 30 days) | Priority | Action | Owner | Due | Success Metric | |----------|--------|-------|-----|----------------| | **P1** | Perform mandrel‑segment clearance inspection (SOP‑MND‑001) | Maintenance Lead (U‑SUP‑02) | **Within 2 days** | Completion & documented clearance values | | **P1** | If clearance out‑of‑tolerance → order expediated DC‑MND‑SEG‑01 and schedule replacement | Procurement & Maintenance | **Within 3 days** (order) / **≤ 1 week** (replacement) | Replacement completed before next coil batch | | **P2** | Verify wrapper‑roll bearing vibration & replace if needed (SOP‑BRG‑001 / SOP‑VIB‑001) | Maintenance Lead (U‑SUP‑01) | **Within 5 days** | Bearing replaced with in‑stock spare; vibration < 1.5 mm/s | | **P2** | Cross‑check process parameters for Alpha‑defect coils (fault DC‑PROC‑001) | Process Engineer (U‑ENG‑02) | **Within 7 days** | No pending coil batches awaiting equipment verification | | **P3** | Initiate oil‑sample analysis and trend wear particles | Maintenance (U‑ENG‑01) | **Within 10 days** | Lab result returned; particle count < 30 ppm | | **P4** | Update spare‑parts inventory (reorder DC‑MND‑SEG‑01; set reorder point for DC‑WRAP‑BRG‑01) | Materials Manager | **Within 14 days** | PO issued; safety stock levels defined | | **P5** | Schedule recurring monitoring (trend charts, vibration alarms) in CMMS | Reliability Engineer | **Within 21 days** | Automated alerts configured; first trend report generated | --- ### Bottom Line * **Immediate safety:** Verify mandrel clearance and bearing health now; replace any out‑of‑spec components using the available spares. * **Predictive control:** Establish a trend‑based wear monitoring programme (clearance, vibration, oil analysis) to replace the mandrel segment set **before** the 70 000 h MTBF is reached. * **Supply security:** Keep at least **one** mandrel‑segment set on hand (6‑week lead) and two wrapper‑roll bearings; implement a dual‑source for the segment set to avoid a 240‑minute production delay. All actions are directly tied to the documented SOPs (SOP‑MND‑001, SOP‑BRG‑001, SOP‑VIB‑001), the fault‑catalog recommendation for DC‑MND‑001, and the current spare‑parts status, ensuring a **data‑driven, actionable maintenance plan** for HSM‑DC‑MND.
